films.title: Batman
reviews: A darkly fascinating film that reinvented the superhero genre. Keaton is surprisingly effective.
Tim Burton brings his gothic sensibility to Gotham and the result is stunning. Keaton owns it.
Keaton was a controversial choice, but he delivers a brooding, haunted Batman unlike any other.
Nicholson steals scenes but Keaton holds the film together with quiet intensity.
A landmark of superhero cinema. Dark, operatic, and visually unforgettable.
The Burton-Keaton Batman is an oddball triumph. Strange, moody, but wholly compelling.
Keaton brings real emotional depth to Bruce Wayne, more so than the costume suggests.
Not perfect but the aesthetic and Keaton's performance make it a classic.

films.title: Batman Returns
reviews: Even more twisted than its predecessor, with Keaton excelling in a darker, weirder world.
Burton doubles down on the grotesque and Keaton matches him beat for beat.
Keaton's Batman feels genuinely tortured here. One of the great superhero performances.
The film belongs to Pfeiffer but Keaton is a rock-solid anchor.
Too dark for some but Keaton shines in moments of vulnerability.
A strange, operatic sequel that polarizes but cannot be ignored.
Keaton is better here than in the original — more confident, more wounded.
The chemistry between Keaton and Pfeiffer elevates an already strong film.

films.title: Batman Forever
reviews: Kilmer has the physique but lacks Keaton's intensity. The film is too cartoonish.
A candy-colored mess but Kilmer is not the problem. He is actually quite decent.
Kilmer brings a smoldering quality to Bruce Wayne that feels fresh but goes underused.
The film betrays Kilmer's potential with neon overload and infantile plotting.
Kilmer is awkward as Batman but oddly compelling as the Bruce Wayne persona.
A step down from Burton's films. Kilmer seems lost in the spectacle.
Despite the surrounding chaos Kilmer delivers a few genuinely good moments.
The franchise loses its soul here but Kilmer is not entirely to blame.

films.title: Batman & Robin
reviews: Clooney himself admitted this film killed the franchise. Batman has a credit card. Enough said.
A catastrophic misfire. Clooney plays Bruce Wayne as a smug caricature.
Even Clooney cannot save this disaster. He looks embarrassed throughout.
The worst superhero film of its era. Clooney is miscast beyond redemption.
Clooney has charisma but absolutely none of it translates into a believable Batman.
A gaudy, deeply stupid film. Clooney deserved better than this debacle.
It is almost impressive how wrong every decision here is including the casting of Clooney.
Clooney apologized for this film publicly. That says everything.

films.title: Batman Begins
reviews: Bale's origin story is gripping, grounded, and emotionally rich. A genuine reboot done right.
The best origin story ever put to film. Bale commands every scene.
Nolan and Bale together reinvent Batman for a new generation. Thrilling and intelligent.
Bale brings a physical ferocity and psychological complexity that previous Batmen lacked.
An exceptional film anchored by Bale's committed, layered performance.
Bale makes Bruce Wayne's journey feel real and earned. Superb work.
The voice is a bit much but Bale delivers the goods where it counts.
Bale gives Batman genuine emotional stakes. This is what the character deserves.

films.title: The Dark Knight
reviews: A masterpiece. Ledger is transcendent but Bale holds the moral center with quiet conviction.
The greatest superhero film ever made. Bale's Batman faces impossible choices and earns our respect.
Bale is asked to be the straight man to Ledger's chaos and does it magnificently.
Bale's physical and emotional restraint is perfect counterbalance to the film's operatic scale.
One of the defining performances of the genre. Bale makes the hero's sacrifice feel real.
Bale never lets the spectacle overshadow the humanity. A landmark performance.
The Dark Knight transcends superhero cinema and Bale is central to why.
Even playing second fiddle to Ledger, Bale's Batman is the soul of this extraordinary film.

films.title: The Dark Knight Rises
reviews: A triumphant conclusion. Bale delivers his most emotionally vulnerable performance of the trilogy.
Bale brings real physical and emotional weight to a broken Bruce Wayne. Deeply moving.
An epic and fitting end. Bale earns every moment of his character's journey.
Bale is extraordinary in conveying Bruce Wayne's exhaustion and eventual rebirth.
The trilogy's close is imperfect but Bale's performance is consistently outstanding.
Bale is the backbone of a flawed but deeply satisfying conclusion.
The most human we have ever seen this Batman. Bale's finest work in the role.
A sprawling blockbuster grounded by Bale's unflinching commitment to the character.

films.title: Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
reviews: Affleck is genuinely the best thing in a bloated mess of a film. He owns every scene.
Despite the film's failures, Affleck's world-weary Batman is magnetic and compelling.
A frustrated, violent Batman done right by Affleck, even if the film itself is a disaster.
Affleck brings gravitas and menace. It is the film around him that disappoints.
The best cast Batman since Keaton, trapped in the wrong movie.
Affleck commands the screen with a brutality and intelligence the role demands.
If any one element works in this mess it is Affleck's tortured, furious Dark Knight.
Affleck is excellent in a film that does not deserve him.

films.title: Justice League
reviews: Affleck looks visibly deflated. The theatrical cut diminishes everything good about his Batman.
A disappointment on every level. Affleck coasts through a film beneath his abilities.
The version of Batman we get here is a pale shadow of what BvS promised.
Affleck is underutilized and clearly disengaged. The production chaos shows.
Even Affleck cannot salvage a film so compromised by studio meddling.
A wasted opportunity. Affleck's Batman deserved a far better team-up film.
The charm is gone. Affleck looks like he wants to be elsewhere.
Reshoots and tonal whiplash undermine Affleck's best instincts for the role.

films.title: Zack Snyder's Justice League
reviews: Affleck's Bat is restored to full power here. Commanding, conflicted, and genuinely heroic.
The Snyder Cut gives Affleck the Batman film he deserved. He is superb throughout.
Affleck shines in the version that actually serves his vision of the character.
A redemption of both the film and Affleck's tenure. Dark, powerful, emotionally rich.
Affleck's Batman finally gets to be the hero of his own story. Worth the wait.
The four-hour runtime vindicates both Snyder's vision and Affleck's performance.
Affleck reminds us why he was so exciting a casting choice in the first place.
The best version of the DCEU Batman. Affleck is compelling from first frame to last.

films.title: The Batman
reviews: Pattinson is raw, damaged, and electric. The most detective-focused Batman ever committed to film.
A revelation. Pattinson strips the character to its wounded core and rebuilds something fresh.
Pattinson's Batman is year two, uncertain, and obsessive. It is the most human iteration yet.
Reeves and Pattinson deliver a neo-noir Batman for the ages. Gripping from start to finish.
Pattinson silences every skeptic. His Bruce Wayne is broken in fascinating ways.
The gloomiest and most emotionally honest Batman film. Pattinson is extraordinary.
A serious, psychologically complex take. Pattinson carries the weight of the film effortlessly.
This Batman feels genuinely dangerous and vulnerable at once. Pattinson is a worthy successor.
